title: Caulking a bath and shower
subtitle: Strip the old, apply the new, tool the bead smooth
excerpt: The complete sequence for re-caulking the joint between a bath or shower tray and the tiled wall: removing the old silicone, cleaning the substrate, applying masking tape guides, gunning a new bead, and tooling it flat.
sourceNotes: Standard UK plumbing and tiling practice. BSI BS 6920 covers suitability of non-metallic materials for water contact. Collins Complete DIY Manual.

glossaryTerms.definition: The continuous strip of sealant applied to a joint. The bead is tooled smooth after application to press it into contact with both surfaces and remove air pockets.

content.text: The silicone joint between a bath rim and the tiled wall is the most commonly neglected joint in a bathroom. When it cracks, darkens with mould, or lifts away from one surface, water gets behind the tiles and into the floor below. Re-caulking takes two hours spread over a day: the bulk of the time is waiting for the surfaces to dry and the fresh silicone to cure. The actual application is fifteen minutes.
What you'll need
Silicone sealant, bathroom grade.
Choose a silicone with an integral fungicide. White is the standard; clear reads well on coloured baths.
Silicone gun.
The standard 400 ml cartridge needs a standard trigger gun. Sold alongside the sealant at builders' merchants.
Silicone remover.
A chemical remover softens old silicone so it can be peeled away cleanly. Mechanical removal alone leaves residue that the new silicone will not bond to.
50 mm scraper and 50 mm filling knife.
For lifting old silicone and trimming the new bead.
Masking tape.
Two strips, one above and one below the joint line. Gives clean edges with no clean-up.
Methylated spirit or isopropyl alcohol.
For wiping down the joint before applying new silicone.
Sanding block, 120-grit.
For removing any silicone residue left after chemical treatment.
Removing the old silicone
Step 1, apply silicone remover.

Squeeze silicone remover along the full length of the old joint and allow it to soak for the time stated on the product (typically 2 to 4 hours). The silicone will soften and can be peeled away in strips.
Step 2, remove the bulk.
Peel the softened silicone away with the filling knife. Work along the joint, not across it, to avoid scoring the acrylic bath. Any silicone left on the tile face can be lifted with a fingernail.
Step 3, sand back the residue.

A thin layer of silicone always remains after mechanical removal. Sand it away with 120-grit on the sanding block. On a steel or cast-iron bath, fine wire wool achieves the same result. On an acrylic bath, use only sandpaper; wire wool scratches the surface.
Step 4, degrease both surfaces.
Wipe along the joint line with a cloth dampened with methylated spirit. Allow 10 minutes to evaporate fully before applying masking tape. Silicone will not bond to a solvent-damp surface.
Applying the new sealant
Step 5, fill the bath with water.

A full bath depresses the acrylic by 5 to 10 mm at the rim. If the sealant is applied while the bath is empty and then the bath fills, the joint is put in tension and will peel away from one surface within a few months. Apply the sealant with the bath full; it then sets in the loaded position.
Step 6, apply masking tape.
Run one strip of masking tape along the tile face about 5 mm above the joint line and one along the bath rim about 5 mm below it. The gap between the two tapes defines the finished bead width.
Step 7, gun the bead.
Cut the nozzle at a 45-degree angle to give a bead roughly 6 mm in diameter. Hold the gun at 45 degrees to the joint and pull it steadily along the full length in one pass. The
bead
should sit on top of the joint rather than filling only the gap; it needs material to tool into the surface on both sides.
Step 8, tool the bead.

Dip a finger in water (or use a purpose-made silicone-finishing tool) and run it along the bead in one continuous pass, pressing lightly to bed the silicone against both surfaces. The goal is a slightly concave profile. Work in one direction and do not go back over a section that has begun to skin.
Step 9, remove the masking tape.

Pull the masking tape away immediately, before the silicone skins. Pull at a low angle, not straight up. Allow the silicone to cure for the time stated on the cartridge (typically 24 hours) before letting water contact the joint.
Variations
Shower tray.
The same sequence applies. A shower tray is not weighted by water the same way, so the fill-before-applying step is less critical, but good practice regardless.
Tile-to-tile joints.

Internal corners between two tiled walls (the vertical inside corners in a shower cubicle) should be silicone, not grouted. Grout cracks at these points because the two tiled surfaces are on different planes and move independently.
Troubleshooter

attrs.symptom: New silicone peels away from the tile face within weeks
attrs.cause: Silicone residue from the old bead was not fully removed; the new silicone bonded to old silicone, not to the tile.
attrs.fix: Remove the new bead completely, sand back further with 120-grit, degrease, and re-apply.

attrs.symptom: Bead looks uneven along the length
attrs.cause: Inconsistent gun speed or gun angle changed mid-pass.
attrs.fix: A single pass at a steady pace gives the best result. Practice the gun speed on a scrap surface before applying to the actual joint. Unevenness can be tooled out by running the damp finger over the bead again immediately.

attrs.symptom: Silicone skins before the masking tape is removed
attrs.cause: Applying sealant in a hot, dry room accelerates skinning.
attrs.fix: Work quickly in sections, removing the tape as each section is tooled. Do not let the tooled section sit for more than a few minutes before tape removal.

attrs.cause: Sealant without a fungicide, or a bathroom with chronic inadequate ventilation.
attrs.fix: Use a fungicide-containing silicone. Address the ventilation: a bathroom extractor fan running for 20 minutes after use keeps the humidity low enough to prevent mould growth.
